trajectory_summary: Holland ran a clean, efficient verify pass on a cooperative customer and hit most qualification checkboxes. The call's structural problem is that it never converted David's genuine bill-pain and environmental motivation into an appointment — it ended as a passive bill-photo request with a Friday callback, which is structurally fragile. The energy David offered at 01:43 was acknowledged but not anchored, and the bill-collection friction at 04:16 was resolved by deferral rather than by working an alternative path.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. The call did not produce a booked appointment — it produced a soft callback promise ('maybe I'll just follow-up on Friday') contingent on a bill photo that David could not provide on the call. This is a callback-with-no-locked-time outcome, not a sit. Per analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, the delta between a locked appointment and 'we'll reconnect Friday' is material to no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Converting a soft callback into a locked appointment with both decision-makers
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Thursday evening? K. So maybe I'll if I don't hear from you, maybe I'll just follow-up on Friday. Would that work?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland ran a complete verify pass on a cooperative, qualified customer and then let the outcome dissolve into a conditional, rep-initiated follow-up with no locked time, no spouse included, and no button-up sequence. The qualification work is done — the only thing missing is the close. This is the highest-leverage gap on this call because it is the gap between a sit and a ghost: David is willing, but 'maybe Friday' with no time and no wife on the line is structurally fragile. Coach Holland on button_up protocol and protocols.spouse_decision as a single combined move: lock the time, confirm the wife, send the text.

key_moments.quote: Hey, David. This is Holland with Ion. So I just got a request that you were looking into solar for your home. How are you doing today?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland skips the legitimacy-establishing intro entirely. Neither the tesla variant nor the standard variant is executed — there is no company framing, no reference to how Ion got David's information, and no credibility anchor in the first 30 seconds. The call opens with a vague 'I just got a request' that does not tell David who Ion is, why they are calling, or why he should trust them. For a cooperative customer this did not trigger resistance, but the codex is explicit: legitimacy in the first 30 seconds is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have.

key_moments.quote: And then I also was kinda curious what got you guys looking into solar?
key_moments.stewart_read: David's answer at 01:22–01:45 is the richest moment in the call. He volunteers two distinct motivations — environmental values ('cleaner and greener') and bill pain ('going up, up, up, up') — with genuine elaboration. This is the emotional anchor the entire pitch should be built around. Holland's response at 01:46 ('the electric bills have been going crazy lately, so I do not blame you guys') acknowledges the bill pain but does not anchor it, does not flip it, and does not tie it forward to the appointment. The environmental motivation is not acknowledged at all. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.

key_moments.quote: K. And then yeah. Right now so we have a pretty good program going on. So what it does is it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar. As long as you have a credit score above 6 70, then you'll qualify. Is Is that the case for you guys?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland transitions directly from bill confirmation ($250/month) to the credit qualifier without anchoring the bill emotionally. The bill is captured as data and dropped. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the capture-and-drop pattern is the universal failure mode across the corpus. The $250 figure David provided is the emotional center of the pitch — it represents what David is paying Dominion every month — and it passes without being named as the problem the design is solving.

key_moments.quote: Last time I checked, it was right around there. It was it was called, I think, the FICO that's FICO usually. Right? That's kind of the Yeah. The short they took. It was late. They don't call it very good. Can't remember if it's, like, 670 or 675, something like that.
key_moments.stewart_read: David's self-reported credit score is explicitly uncertain — he cannot confirm whether it is 670 or 675, and he is recalling from memory. Holland qualifies him at 03:07 ('you guys would qualify, which is great news') without any hedge. Per Spencer's operator note in qualifiers.credit_qualifier, stated credit is unreliable — customers see Vantage scores, not FICO; three bureaus often differ by 30–50 points. Holland is making a qualification decision on a number David himself cannot confirm. If the actual pulled score comes in below 670, this qualification is a false positive that wastes the closer's time and potentially the setter's sit.
